foreach ($item in $items) {
    if (Test-Path $item.FullName) {
        $delete = $false
        if ($item.PSIsContainer) {
            if ($dirNames -contains $item.Name) {
                $delete = $true
                $counts.DirPatterns++
            }
        } else {
            if ($fileExtensions -contains $item.Extension -or $fileNames -contains $item.Name) {
                $delete = $true
                $counts.FilePatterns++
            } else {
                foreach ($pat in $wildcardFiles) {
                    if ($item.Name -like $pat) {
                        $delete = $true
                        $counts.FilePatterns++
                        break
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        if ($delete) {
            Write-Output "Deleting: $($item.FullName)"
            Remove-Item -Path $item.FullName -Recurse -Force -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
        }
    }
}

Write-Output "`nDeletion Summary:"
Write-Output "Directories deleted: $($counts.DirPatterns)"
Write-Output "Files deleted: $($counts.FilePatterns)"

foreach ($pair in $filesToRename) {
    $old = $pair[0]
    $new = $pair[1]
    
    # In Windows PowerShell, Test-Path is case-insensitive by default.
    # However, Rename-Item might fail if naming to the same thing in a case-insensitive way if we don't watch out.
    # But since these are different names (hyphens vs underscores), it should be fine.
    
    if (Test-Path $old) {
        # Check if the case/name is already the target name.
        # Get-Item helps us get the exact case from the filesystem.
        $actualFile = Get-Item $old
        if ($actualFile.Name -ne (Split-Path $new -Leaf)) {
             Write-Output "Renaming $old to $new"
             Rename-Item -Path $old -NewName (Split-Path $new -Leaf) -Force
        } else {
             Write-Output "$old already exists with correct target name (or case-insensitive match)."
        }
    }
}

Write-Output "`nExisting target paths:"
foreach ($pair in $filesToRename) {
    if (Test-Path $pair[1]) {
        Write-Output $pair[1]
    }
}

I’m standardizing backend service module names now (`snake_case` -> `kebab-case`) and automatically rewriting all `require/import` paths in backend JS files to match.

Execution complete: Rename backend service files and rewrite imports

Ran terminal command: $mapping = @{}
$filesUnderServices = Get-ChildItem -Path "src/services" -Filter "*_*.js"
foreach ($file in $filesUnderServices) {
    $oldName = $file.Name
    $newName = $oldName.Replace('_', '-')
    $mapping[$oldName.Replace('.js', '')] = $newName.Replace('.js', '')
    Rename-Item -Path $file.FullName -NewName $newName
}

$allJsFiles = Get-ChildItem -Path "src" -Filter "*.js" -Recurse
foreach ($file in $allJsFiles) {
    $content = Get-Content -Path $file.FullName -Raw
    $changed = $false
    foreach ($oldBase in $mapping.Keys) {
        $newBase = $mapping[$oldBase]
        # Replace occurrences of the module name. 
        # Using regex to ensure we are likely hitting module paths/requires.
        if ($content -match $oldBase) {
            $content = $content -replace [regex]::Escape($oldBase), $newBase
            $changed = $true
        }
    }
    if ($changed) {
        Set-Content -Path $file.FullName -Value $content
    }
}

Write-Host "Mapping Table (Old -> New):"
$mapping.GetEnumerator() | Select-Object @{Name='Old';Expression={$_.Key}}, @{Name='New';Expression={$_.Value}} | Format-Table

Write-Host "`nRemaining matches for 'services/.*_' or 'require\(.*_.*\)' in src/**/*.js:"
Get-ChildItem -Path "src" -Filter "*.js" -Recurse | Select-String -Pattern "services/.*_", "require\(.*_.*\)" | Select-Object Path, LineNumber, Line | Format-Table


Ran terminal command: $mapping = @{}
$filesUnderServices = Get-ChildItem -Path "src/services" -Filter "*_*.js"
foreach ($file in $filesUnderServices) {
    $oldName = $file.Name
    $newName = $oldName.Replace('_', '-')
    $mapping[$oldName.Replace('.js', '')] = $newName.Replace('.js', '')
    Rename-Item -Path $file.FullName -NewName $newName
}

$allJsFiles = Get-ChildItem -Path "src" -Filter "*.js" -Recurse
foreach ($file in $allJsFiles) {
    $content = Get-Content -Path $file.FullName -Raw
    $changed = $false
    foreach ($oldBase in $mapping.Keys) {
        $newBase = $mapping[$oldBase]
        if ($content -match $oldBase) {
            $content = $content -replace [regex]::Escape($oldBase), $newBase
            $changed = $true
        }
    }
    if ($changed) {
        Set-Content -Path $file.FullName -Value $content
    }
}

    $lineNum = 0

        $lineNum++
        if ($line -match "require\s*\(\s*['""](\.?\./.*?)['""]\s*\)") {
            $relPath = $Matches[1]
            $baseDir = Split-Path $file.FullName
            $targetPath = [System.IO.Path]::GetFullPath([System.IO.Path]::Combine($baseDir, $relPath))
            
            $exists = $false
            if (Test-Path $targetPath -PathType Leaf) {
                $exists = $true
            } elseif (Test-Path "$targetPath.js" -PathType Leaf) {
                $exists = $true
            } elseif (Test-Path (Join-Path $targetPath "index.js") -PathType Leaf) {
                $exists = $true
            }
            
            if (-not $exists) {
                Write-Output "Missing target: $($file.FullName):$lineNum -> $relPath"
                $missingCount++
            }
        }
    }
}

if ($missingCount -eq 0) {
    Write-Output "No missing local require targets"
}

            $found = $true
        }

            Write-Host "MISSING: $relPath required in $file"
            $global:missingCount++
        }
    }

GitHub Copilot: Yes, your Gemini API key file should be ignored in current rules, depending on filename/location:

- .env and `.env.*` are ignored globally and in backend, so keys inside env files are ignored.
- Files inside `secrets/` or `credentials/` folders are ignored.
- Any `*.key` file is ignored.

There is no explicit `gemini`-named rule, but generic secret patterns already cover common Gemini key file setups.

User: I've completed the SQL schema review and found a critical issue.

Critical Finding: Policy Coverage Period Mismatch
The policies table has coverage_end defaulting to 7 days, but the README specifies a 91-day (13-week) quarterly commitment.

Current SQL (line 139 in both schema files):

sql
coverage_end DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '7 days',
Required SQL:

sql
coverage_end DATE NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_DATE + INTERVAL '91 days',
The README states: "Quarterly commitment: Plans and add-ons are quarterly (13-week) commitments, not weekly toggles"

This mismatch means:

Policies are created with only 7-day coverage instead of required 91-day lock period
The UI showing "Policy locked for 91 days" would be inconsistent with actual database values
Users could potentially renew/cancel policies earlier than intended

This prevents stale legacy constraints from triggering operator does not exist: policy_tier_enum = text.

Run this once immediately in Supabase SQL Editor to unblock current environment (if you are mid-migration on an already-mutated DB):

DO $$
DECLARE
  c RECORD;
BEGIN
  FOR c IN
    SELECT conname
    FROM pg_constraint
    WHERE conrelid = 'public.policies'::regclass
      AND contype = 'c'
      AND pg_get_constraintdef(oid) ILIKE '%plan_tier%'
  LOOP
    EXECUTE format('ALTER TABLE policies DROP CONSTRAINT IF EXISTS %I', c.conname);
  END LOOP;
END $$;

After that, rerun the remediation section from hustlr_consolidated_schema.sql. If you want, I can also add a tiny preflight query block that prints all remaining plan_tier-related constraints before migration starts.

$connections = Get-NetTCPConnection -LocalPort $port -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
if ($connections) {
    echo "Port $port active connections:";
    foreach ($c in $connections) {
        $proc = Get-Process -Id $c.OwningProcess -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue;
        echo "PID: $($c.OwningProcess), State: $($c.State), Process: $($proc.ProcessName)";
    }

    foreach ($url in $urls) {
        try {
            $resp = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -TimeoutSec 2 -UseBasicParsing;
            echo "$url status: $($resp.StatusCode)";
        } catch {
            echo "$url status: $($_.Exception.Message)";
        }
    }
} else {
    echo "No listener found on port $port.";
}

User: The 1.1 GB inside .git/objects is split between two types of data:

Pack Files (~0.56 GB): These are compressed archives of your project's history.
Loose Objects (~0.5 GB): You have hundreds of small directories (like 35/, fa/, f1/, etc.) containing "loose" objects. These are created every time you stage a file or make a commit before Git has had a chance to compress them into a pack file.
Having 0.5 GB of loose objects is quite a lot and usually happens after a period of intense development or when large files are frequently modified.

How to fix it:
You can consolidate and compress all these loose objects into the pack files by running:

powershell
git gc --prune=now --aggressive
This command will:

Compress loose objects into a single pack file.
Remove any objects that are no longer needed.
Optimize the local repository database.
Would you like me to run this for you? (It may take 1–2 minutes given the size).

12:03 PM
??


GitHub Copilot: You’re right, and your diagnosis is accurate. I’ll run a safe local Git cleanup now (`git gc --prune=now --aggressive`) and report the before/after .git size so you can see the exact gain.

Execution complete: Run git garbage collection and report size savings

Ran terminal command: $gitPath = Join-Path (Get-Location) ".git";
$gitObjectsPath = Join-Path $gitPath "objects";

function Get-Size($path) {
    if (Test-Path $path) {
        $size = (Get-ChildItem -Path $path -File -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum;
        return [Math]::Round($size / 1MB, 2);
    }
    return 0;
}

$beforeTotal = Get-Size $gitPath;
$beforeObjects = Get-Size $gitObjectsPath;

Write-Host "Before: Total .git: $beforeTotal MB, .git/objects: $beforeObjects MB";

git gc --prune=now --aggressive;

$afterTotal = Get-Size $gitPath;
$afterObjects = Get-Size $gitObjectsPath;

Write-Host "After: Total .git: $afterTotal MB, .git/objects: $afterObjects MB";
git count-objects -vH | Select-String "count:|size:|in-pack:|packs:|size-pack:|prune-packable:"

Ran terminal command: function Get-DirSize($path) {
    $size = (Get-ChildItem -Path $path -File -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum;
    return [Math]::Round(($size / 1MB), 2);
}

$beforeTotal = Get-DirSize ".git";
$beforeObjects = Get-DirSize ".git/objects";
Write-Host "BEFORE_TOTAL: $beforeTotal MB";
Write-Host "BEFORE_OBJECTS: $beforeObjects MB";

git gc --prune=now --aggressive;

$afterTotal = Get-DirSize ".git";
$afterObjects = Get-DirSize ".git/objects";
Write-Host "AFTER_TOTAL: $afterTotal MB";
Write-Host "AFTER_OBJECTS: $afterObjects MB";

git count-objects -vH | Select-String "count|size|in-pack|packs|size-pack|prune-packable"

User: what is the use of lfs ?

GitHub Copilot: Git LFS (Large File Storage) is used to handle large/binary files in Git repos without bloating normal Git history.

What it does:
1. Replaces large files in Git history with tiny pointer files.
2. Stores the real file contents in LFS storage (separate from normal Git objects).
3. Downloads actual binaries only when needed during checkout/pull.

Why teams use it:
1. Keeps clone/fetch of code faster for normal development.
2. Prevents Git repo size from exploding with large binaries.
3. Better for assets like models, datasets, videos, APKs, PSDs, etc.

Typical good candidates for LFS:
1. ML model binaries (`.pkl`, `.onnx`, `.pt`, `.bin`)
2. Large datasets (`.csv`, `.parquet`) if versioned intentionally
3. Build artifacts (`.apk`, `.ipa`) when you truly want them in version control

Important tradeoff:
1. LFS has bandwidth/storage quotas (especially on free plans).
2. If quota is exceeded, fresh clone/checkout can fail with smudge errors, like you saw on Render.

Rule of thumb:
1. Source code and config: normal Git.
2. Big reproducible/generated artifacts: usually don’t commit; keep in object storage/releases.
3. Big must-version binaries: use LFS, but monitor quota.

In your case, removing huge deploy-time artifacts from LFS/Git was correct for Render reliability.

User: do we need it even in git?

GitHub Copilot: Short answer: no, you do not need Git LFS for this repo right now.

Given your current setup:
1. Render deploys were breaking because of LFS quota.
2. You already removed the problematic LFS-tracked files.
3. These files are generated/heavy artifacts, not core source code.

So for your project, better approach:
1. Keep code, configs, SQL, and lightweight fixtures in normal Git.
2. Keep large datasets/models/APKs out of Git (and out of LFS unless absolutely required).
3. Store heavy artifacts in release/object storage if needed (S3/GCS/Drive/Render disk, etc.).

When you should use LFS:
1. You must version large binary assets in Git history.
2. Team needs exact binary versions tied to commits.
3. You can maintain enough LFS quota reliably.

For you now: staying without LFS is the safer choice for CI/CD reliability.

foreach ($url in $endpoints) {
    Write-Host "Checking URL: $url"
    try {
        $response = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -Method Get -TimeoutSec 60 -ErrorAction Stop -UseBasicParsing
        Write-Host "Status Code: $($response.StatusCode)"
        Write-Host "Body: $($response.Content.Substring(0, [Math]::Min(300, $response.Content.Length)))"
    } catch {
        Write-Host "Error accessing $url"
        if ($_.Exception.Response) {
            Write-Host "Status Code: $($_.Exception.Response.StatusCode.value__)"
            $reader = New-Object System.IO.StreamReader($_.Exception.Response.GetResponseStream())
            $body = $reader.ReadToEnd()
            Write-Host "Body: $($body.Substring(0, [Math]::Min(300, $body.Length)))"
        } else {
            Write-Host "Error: $($_.Exception.Message)"
        }
    }
    Write-Host "-----------------------------------"
}

Ran terminal command: $endpoints = @(

$results = foreach ($url in $endpoints) {
    try {
        $response = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -Method Get -TimeoutSec 60 -ErrorAction Stop -UseBasicParsing
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            URL = $url
            Status = $response.StatusCode
            Body = $response.Content.Substring(0, [Math]::Min(300, $response.Content.Length))
        }
    } catch {
        $statusCode = if ($_.Exception.Response) { $_.Exception.Response.StatusCode.value__ } else { "Error" }
        $body = if ($_.Exception.Response) { 
            $reader = New-Object System.IO.StreamReader($_.Exception.Response.GetResponseStream())
            $content = $reader.ReadToEnd()
            $content.Substring(0, [Math]::Min(300, $content.Length))
        } else { $_.Exception.Message }
        
        [PSCustomObject]@{
            URL = $url
            Status = $statusCode
            Body = $body
        }
    }
}

Found and fixed the actual reason your Admin Console was stuck on “Failed to load data”.

What was happening:
1. In live mode, admin API calls were still pointing to localhost in api-service.ts, which fails in production.
2. Your backend health endpoints are up, but two queue endpoints currently return 500:
   - /api/admin/fraud-queue
   - /api/admin/payout-queue
3. Because loading used one all-or-nothing promise, those 500s caused the whole dashboard to fail.

What I changed:
1. Switched admin live API base to the production constant in api-service.ts.
2. Made dashboard loading resilient in AdminContext.tsx:
   - Core data (analytics + system health) must load.
   - Fraud/payout queue failures now degrade to empty lists instead of crashing the page.

import 'dart:convert';
import 'dart:developer' as developer;
import 'dart:io';
import 'package:flutter/foundation.dart' show kIsWeb, kReleaseMode;
import 'package:http/http.dart' as http;

import 'package:google_mlkit_face_detection/google_mlkit_face_detection.dart';
import 'storage_service.dart';
import '../core/secrets.dart';

class ApiServiceException implements Exception {
  final String message;
  final int statusCode;
  ApiServiceException(this.message, this.statusCode);

  @override
  String toString() => message;
}

class ApiService {
  /// **Production (like Swiggy / Facebook):** users install one app; it talks to **your cloud API**
  /// (Node on AWS/GCP/Azure, ML as an internal service â€” never on the phone). You bake the prod URL
  /// into the release binary with `--dart-define=HUSTLR_API_PROD=https://api.yourdomain.com`.
  ///
  /// **Local dev:** `--dart-define=HUSTLR_API_BASE=...` or repo [scripts/start-dev.ps1].
  // Production Render URL
  static const _prodUrl = 'https://hustlr-ad32.onrender.com';

  static String get baseUrl {
    const prod = String.fromEnvironment('HUSTLR_API_PROD');
    const devOverride = String.fromEnvironment('HUSTLR_API_BASE');
    
    if (kIsWeb) {
      if (prod.isNotEmpty) return prod;
      if (devOverride.isNotEmpty) return devOverride;
      return _prodUrl;
    }
    
    // In production builds, prioritize prod environment variable, then hardcoded prod URL
    if (kReleaseMode) {
      return prod.isNotEmpty ? prod : _prodUrl;
    }
    
    // In debug mode, allow overrides, but ensure regular devices don't see empty host
    if (devOverride.isNotEmpty) return devOverride;
    if (prod.isNotEmpty) return prod;

    // Safe default on physical devices when no dart-defines are passed.
    return _prodUrl;
  }

  static const _timeout =
      Duration(seconds: 60); // 60s â€” accommodates Render free tier cold starts

  static const _googleVisionApiKey = Secrets.googleVisionApiKey;

  static final ApiService instance = ApiService._internal();
  ApiService._internal();

  static String get mlBackendUrl {
    const prod = String.fromEnvironment(
      'HUSTLR_ML_PROD',
      defaultValue: 'https://hustlr-2ppj.onrender.com',
    );
    const devOverride = String.fromEnvironment('HUSTLR_ML_BASE');
    if (devOverride.isNotEmpty) return devOverride;
    return prod;
  }

  String? currentUserId;
  String? currentPolicyId;
  String? accessToken;

  Future<String> _effectiveUserZone() async {
    return await StorageService.instance.getUserZone() ??
        StorageService.getString('workerZone') ??
        'Unknown Zone';
  }

  Future<String> _effectiveUserCity() async {

$commits = @("4a6a48e", "fe80da6", "b3d6cb8", "HEAD")

foreach ($file in $files) {
    Write-Host "`n--- Analysis for $file ---"
    
    # 1) Which commits changed it
    Write-Host "Change History (among 4a6a48e, fe80da6, b3d6cb8):"
    git log --format="%h" 4a6a48e fe80da6 b3d6cb8 -- $file | Select-Object -Unique
    
    # 2) Compare HEAD with the commits
    foreach ($commit in $commits[0..2]) {
        $diff = git diff HEAD $commit -- $file
        if (-not $diff) {
            Write-Host "HEAD matches $commit"
        }
    }

    # 3) Check content in each commit
    foreach ($commit in ("b3d6cb8", "fe80da6", "4a6a48e")) {
        $content = git show "${commit}:${file}" 2>$null
        if ($content -and $content.Trim().Length -gt 0) {
            Write-Host "Most recent valid commit among the three: $commit"
            break
        }
    }
}
